course.title: CSS Mastery
course.description: Level up your CSS skills — flexbox, grid, animations, custom properties, and modern layout patterns.

content.beginner:
Every element in CSS is a box. The box has four layers:
- **Content** — the actual text/image
- **Padding** — space inside the border
- **Border** — the outline
- **Margin** — space outside the border

Think of it like a framed picture: content → mat → frame → wall space.
content.intermediate: The default `box-sizing: content-box` means `width` only covers content — padding and border add to the total size. Use `box-sizing: border-box` (in a reset) so `width` includes padding and border — much more predictable.
content.advanced: Margin collapsing: vertical margins between adjacent block elements collapse to the largest single margin. Margins of a parent and first/last child collapse if no border, padding, or formatting context separates them. Flexbox and Grid containers don't collapse margins with their children.

content.intermediate:
Key flex properties:
- `flex-direction`: row/column
- `flex-wrap`: wrap to next line
- `justify-content`: main axis alignment (space-between, around, evenly)
- `align-items`: cross axis alignment
- `flex: 1` on children = 'grow to fill available space'
- `flex-shrink: 0` = 'don't shrink'
content.advanced: The `flex` shorthand is `flex-grow flex-shrink flex-basis`. `flex: 1` = `1 1 0%` (equal-width flexible children). `flex: auto` = `1 1 auto` (flexible, respects content size). `flex: none` = `0 0 auto` (rigid). `align-self` overrides `align-items` for a single child.

content.intermediate: `fr` units distribute space after fixed sizes. Named lines: `grid-template-columns: [sidebar-start] 250px [sidebar-end main-start] 1fr [main-end]`. Template areas: a visual ASCII-art layout. `auto-fill` vs `auto-fit` with `minmax`: `repeat(auto-fill, minmax(200px, 1fr))` for responsive columns without media queries.
content.advanced: Explicit vs implicit grid: you define the explicit grid with `grid-template-*`; items overflowing create the implicit grid (controlled by `grid-auto-rows/columns`). Subgrid (`grid-template-columns: subgrid`) lets children participate in the parent grid's track definition — great for aligning content across cards.

content.intermediate: Custom properties cascade and are inherited (unlike preprocessor variables). They can be updated with JavaScript: `el.style.setProperty('--color', '#fff')`. They can have fallbacks: `var(--maybe-undefined, blue)`. They're scoped to the element they're declared on and all descendants.
content.advanced: Custom properties are powerful for theming, component APIs, and calculated values. They participate in transitions/animations (with `@property` for registered custom properties). `@property` lets you declare syntax, inheritance, and initial value — enabling animations on non-interpolatable values like colors stored in variables.

content.intermediate: `transition` shorthand: `property duration timing-function delay`. Transition multiple properties: `transition: color 0.2s, transform 0.3s`. Prefer transitioning `transform` and `opacity` — they're GPU-accelerated and don't cause layout recalculation. Avoid transitioning `width`, `height`, or `margin`.
content.advanced: Transitions require a start and end state — they only animate between two values. For more complex animations, use `@keyframes`. The `will-change: transform` hint promotes an element to its own compositor layer (use sparingly — excessive use wastes GPU memory). `prefers-reduced-motion` media query: wrap animations in `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference)`.

content.intermediate: `animation` shorthand: `name duration timing-function delay iteration-count direction fill-mode`. Key values: `animation-fill-mode: forwards` (keep end state), `animation-direction: alternate` (ping-pong), `animation-play-state: paused` (pause via JS). Use `from`/`to` as aliases for `0%`/`100%`.
content.advanced: Keyframe animations run on the compositor thread for `transform` and `opacity` — no main thread needed. CSS `animation-timeline` (scroll-driven animations, CSS 2023) links animation progress to scroll position without JavaScript: `animation-timeline: scroll()`. Use `View Transition API` for page-transition animations.

content.intermediate: Mobile-first approach: write base styles for small screens, override for larger ones using `min-width`. This is more efficient than desktop-first with `max-width`. Use `clamp()` for fluid typography: `font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 1.5rem)`. CSS Grid with `auto-fill`/`auto-fit` can eliminate many media queries.
content.advanced: Container queries (`@container`) are now widely supported — apply styles based on the container's size, not the viewport. Use `container-type: inline-size` on the parent. Combined with `:has()` selector, you can build fully self-contained, context-aware components that don't need JavaScript for responsive behavior.

content.beginner:
When two CSS rules conflict, specificity decides the winner. Think of it as a scoring system:
- Element selector (p, div): 1 point
- Class (.button): 10 points
- ID (#header): 100 points
- Inline style: 1000 points

Higher score wins!
content.intermediate: Specificity is calculated as (A, B, C): A = IDs, B = classes/attributes/pseudo-classes, C = elements/pseudo-elements. Equal specificity: last rule wins (cascade order). `!important` overrides everything (avoid it — it breaks the natural cascade). `:is()`, `:not()`, `:has()` take the specificity of their most specific argument.
content.advanced: CSS Cascade Layers (`@layer`) give you explicit control over cascade order, superseding specificity: rules in later-declared layers win regardless of specificity. This solves the specificity war problem in large codebases. `@layer utilities { }` at the end beats all unlayered styles — the opposite of what you might expect.

content.beginner:
CSS has come a long way! Some cool modern features:
- `:is()` — matches any of a list of selectors
- `gap` — works in both flexbox AND grid
- `aspect-ratio` — maintain proportions easily
- `clamp()` — fluid sizing without media queries
- Logical properties (`padding-inline`, `margin-block`)
content.intermediate:
2023-2024 CSS highlights:
- `@layer` cascade layers
- `@container` container queries
- `:has()` parent selector (finally!)
- `color-mix()` for blending colors
- `text-wrap: balance` for better headlines
- `@starting-style` for entry animations
- Nesting (no preprocessor needed!)
content.advanced:
Cutting edge:
- `scroll-driven animations` (no JS needed for scroll effects)
- `View Transitions API` (page animations)
- `Anchor Positioning` (tooltips/popovers without JS)
- `CSS Houdini` (custom paint worklets)
- `@property` registered custom properties with animation support
- `light-dark()` for automatic dark mode

content.beginner:
Look at you! You've learned:
✅ Box Model
✅ Flexbox
✅ CSS Grid
✅ Custom Properties
✅ Transitions & Animations
✅ Responsive Design

Next: try building a real layout project. Recreate a website you love!
content.intermediate:
Solid CSS skills! Explore further:
- **Tailwind CSS** — utility-first framework
- **CSS Modules** — scoped styles in React
- **Styled Components** or **CSS-in-JS**
- **Storybook** for design systems
- **Motion One** for performant animations
content.advanced:
You've got depth. Next:
- Design system architecture
- CSS performance profiling
- Custom @property animations
- Scroll-driven animation patterns
- CSS Architecture methodologies (CUBE, ITCSS)

options: Adds a border to all elements
Makes width include padding and border
questions.explanation: border-box makes the width property include padding and border, so the element is exactly the size you set — much more predictable than the default content-box.

questions.questionText: What does `justify-content: space-between` do in flexbox?
options: Distributes items with space between them, first and last at edges
questions.explanation: space-between places the first item at the start, last at the end, and distributes remaining items with equal space between them.

questions.questionText: What does `1fr` mean in CSS Grid?
questions.explanation: fr is a fractional unit. 1fr means 'take 1 equal share of the available space'. Three columns of 1fr each would each get 1/3 of the space.

questions.questionText: Why should you prefer transitioning `transform` over `width`?
options: transform is GPU-accelerated and doesn't cause layout recalculation
questions.explanation: transform and opacity are handled by the GPU compositor and don't trigger layout or paint — making them much smoother, especially on mobile.

questions.questionText: In mobile-first CSS, what does the base style target?
questions.explanation: Mobile-first means base styles are written for small screens, then you add overrides with min-width media queries for larger screens. This is more efficient than max-width desktop-first.

questions.questionText: Which CSS selector has the HIGHEST specificity?
questions.explanation: IDs (#submit) have a specificity of (1,0,0) — higher than classes (0,1,0) or elements (0,0,1).
